Q1: My study routine was honestly chaotic most of the time. I'd make these elaborate schedules at the start of each week, color-coded and everything because I'm very visual, but then life would get in the way. I'd end up cramming around 23 hours into random chunks, often late at night when my anxiety was keeping me awake anyway. I tried to be organized, I really did, but between my extracurriculars and just feeling overwhelmed, the routine rarely stuck for more than a few days.
Q2: It varied wildly depending on deadlines and honestly, depending on how scared I was feeling about failing. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, and then panic would set in and I'd try to do everything at once. The inconsistency made everything harder because I never built momentum. When deadlines piled up, I'd abandon any pretense of a schedule and just react to whatever was most urgent.
Q3: Usually fear prompted me to log in, if I'm being honest. I'd get this wave of anxiety thinking about what I might be missing or what deadline was approaching. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were new announcements that might affect my grade. The peer support group I was part of would sometimes remind each other to check the LMS, which helped, but mostly it was dread that drove me there.
Q4: There was a brief period mid-semester when logging in became more routine, mostly because I'd connected with a few classmates through the peer support program. We'd coordinate online and that gave me a reason to check in regularly. But that routine fell apart when assignments started piling up and I got too overwhelmed to even face the platform. The habit never really solidified because my anxiety would spike whenever I saw how behind I was.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful for me because I learn best visually. I watched over 150 minutes of content because seeing concepts demonstrated made them click in ways reading couldn't. The discussion boards were also useful when I actually participated because hearing other students' perspectives helped me feel less alone in my confusion. Those 15 posts I made were some of my most productive learning moments.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and some of the practice assignments. The readings felt endless and my attention would drift, so I'd tell myself I'd get to them later. The practice problems I avoided because they made me feel stupid when I couldn't solve them, which fed into my anxiety. I know that's counterproductive, but when you're already scared of failing, voluntarily confronting your weaknesses feels impossible.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate relevance or when it connected to something visual I could grasp. If I watched a video and suddenly understood a concept, that felt valuable. Discussions with peers where we actually helped each other also felt worthwhile. But anything that felt like busywork or that I couldn't see the point of immediately, I'd deprioritize, even if logically I knew it mattered.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. Required ones I'd stress about constantly, but optional ones created this guilt spiral where I knew I should do them but couldn't bring myself to add more to my plate. The required ones at least had clear consequences, which paradoxically made them easier to prioritize even though they stressed me out more. Optional activities just became another thing I was failing at.
Q9: Yes, absolutely. I'd download lecture slides and videos to study offline, partly because my internet connection was more reliable that way and partly because seeing the LMS reminded me of everything I hadn't done. I'd spread printouts across my desk and annotate them heavily with colored markers. I also used external YouTube videos to supplement concepts I didn't understand from the course materials.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be at my desk for hours with printed materials, my notebook full of diagrams and visual summaries, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd rewatch downloaded videos multiple times, pausing and sketching out concepts. My study group would meet at a café sometimes and we'd work through problems together on paper. None of that showed up anywhere in the system.
Q11: Constantly. I'd access a video or PDF once, download it, and then that would be my primary study material for days. The LMS would show one brief login, but I'd have spent hours with that same content offline. It was actually my preferred way to study because I could control the environment better and not get distracted by notifications or reminders of other overdue work.
Q12: Around midterms, everything became harder. I had multiple deadlines converging, my extracurricular commitments were demanding more time, and I started feeling genuinely scared about my performance. The anxiety became almost paralyzing. I'd open the LMS, see the mountain of incomplete tasks, and just close it again. That's when my attendance really dropped and I started falling behind in ways I couldn't recover from.
Q13: All of those factors hit me hard. The workload felt insurmountable, which created confusion about where to even start. Fatigue from anxious nights made it hard to concentrate during the day. My extracurricular commitments, which I couldn't drop, competed for the same limited energy. When I did use the LMS under those conditions, I'd often just skim things without really absorbing anything.
Q14: More times than I want to admit. I'd have the video playing but be too exhausted or anxious to actually process what was being said. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd open an assignment, stare at it for twenty minutes feeling increasingly panicked, and then close it without typing anything. The activity was there, but the engagement wasn't.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even ones I knew were crucial for my grade. The fear of doing them poorly was somehow worse than the fear of not doing them at all, which makes no sense but that's how my brain worked. I'd tell myself I needed to understand the material better first, but that preparation never happened. By the time I couldn't delay anymore, I'd rush through and submit subpar work.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that actually got me to submit anything. Without them, I'd have turned in nothing. But they also created these intense stress spikes where I'd cram everything into the last possible hours. My response time averaging over 22 hours shows how I'd wait until the pressure was unbearable. The deadline-driven approach meant I never had time to actually learn deeply.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. When I got positive feedback, it motivated me briefly, but negative feedback sent me into a spiral where I'd avoid that type of assignment even more. The peer support helped buffer some of that because my study partners would help me interpret feedback constructively. But honestly, I often didn't even check feedback because I was too scared of what it might say.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming from the start. There were so many components and the expectations weren't always clear to me visually. I needed more roadmaps, more visual guides showing how everything connected. The weekly modules made sense in theory, but when I fell behind, the structure became a reminder of failure rather than a helpful framework. It wasn't designed for someone who might struggle to keep up.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because it matched my learning style. But the sheer volume of reading and text-based materials made it harder. I also found the assignment submission process confusing sometimes, which added unnecessary friction. If there had been more visual progress trackers or clearer milestone markers, I might have stayed more on track. The design assumed a level of organization I couldn't maintain.
Q20: I think that was literally me. I'd log in frequently, watch videos, participate in discussions, but then freeze when it came to actually submitting work. The submission part requires confidence that your work is good enough, and I never felt that. High activity can mean someone is trying desperately to understand and engage, but the final step of producing something feels too risky. Fear of judgment is real.
Q21: I've seen classmates who barely logged in but did well because they studied efficiently offline or already had strong foundations. They didn't need the LMS to learn. For me, it was the opposite—I needed more support than the LMS could show, but I also did a lot of invisible studying that didn't translate to performance because my test anxiety was so high. Low activity doesn't always mean low effort.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and timing pretty accurately—when someone logs in, how long they spend on videos. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement, the emotional state behind it, and all the offline work. They can't tell if someone watched a video three times because they were confused or because they were thorough. They miss fear, effort, and struggle entirely.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and putting genuine effort into learning, even when it's hard. It means showing up mentally, not just physically or digitally. For me, I was engaged in the sense that I desperately wanted to succeed and spent hours trying, but my engagement was undermined by anxiety and poor execution. Engagement should include the intention and effort, not just the outcomes.
Q24: The LMS couldn't show how many nights I stayed up worrying about this course, or how much I actually learned through peer conversations that happened outside the platform. It couldn't capture the hours I spent with printed materials and hand-drawn diagrams. It couldn't show that my low assignment completion wasn't from lack of caring but from paralyzing fear of failure. The data shows a failing student, but it misses someone who was trying incredibly hard and just couldn't make it work.
